judgments.comparative_verdict: Comparable — both reports hit all four expectations; Report 2 is slightly more precise on the metadata allowlist, while Report 1 adds extra defects, but the graded substance is equivalent.

report_1.evidence: Because the handler catches the base `Exception`, **all six are treated identically** — logged as a warning and retried once on the secondary vendor. ... `BadRequestError` — *inappropriate*. ... `ContentPolicyError` — *inappropriate and risky*. ... `AuthError` — *inappropriate*. ... branch on exception type — retry only transient classes (`RateLimitError`/`RequestTimeoutError`/`ServerError`), re-raise or return a structured error for `BadRequestError`/`ContentPolicyError`/`AuthError`

report_2.evidence: It catches `Exception` — the single broadest class — so **every** error class the primary can raise ... is handled identically: log a warning and resend the same payload to the secondary vendor. ... `BadRequestError`, `ContentPolicyError`, `AuthError`: failing over is **wrong**. These are definitively non-retryable. ... replace the blanket catch with an explicit non-retryable denylist, e.g. NON_RETRYABLE = (BadRequestError, ContentPolicyError, AuthError)

report_1.evidence: `if os.environ.get("STAGE") != "production": return True` ... rate limiting is silently disabled outside a precisely-named stage. ... `except RedisError: return True` — if Redis errors, allow. *Assessment:* **fail-open**. A Redis outage disables all rate limiting silently ... failing closed (or at least alerting) is usually the safer default

report_2.evidence: Returns True whenever `STAGE` is anything other than the literal `"production"` ... This gates fail-open on an environment-name check ... It should fail *closed* by default ... **Path C** — `except RedisError: return True`. If Redis is unavailable, the limiter **fails open** ... the safety gate should fail *closed* when its backing store is down. Fix: `return False` on `RedisError`

report_1.evidence: the **full `input_text` of the intake document** into the application log at INFO. Since the documents contain sensitive personal information, this dumps PII directly into logs ... do **not** log `input_text` at all (log a length/hash or redacted summary if needed); scrub `metadata` for embedded PII

report_2.evidence: **`input_text` is logged verbatim** — the full intake document text (the sensitive PII itself) goes into the application logs on every call. ... **`metadata` is dumped wholesale** ... log metadata by an explicit **allowlist** of safe keys and drop everything else ... drop `input_text` or replace it with a truncated hash ... replace `record.metadata` with `{k: record.metadata[k] for k in ALLOWED_KEYS if k in record.metadata}`

report_1.evidence: if `PII_SALT` is **unset or empty, it silently defaults to `""`**, so the output becomes `sha256(applicant_id)` — an unsalted hash. ... there is no error, warning, or startup check to signal this ... require `PII_SALT` explicitly (raise/refuse to start if unset or empty) rather than defaulting to `""`

report_2.evidence: **When the dependency is unsatisfied it silently falls back to an empty string salt** (`""` default), producing an *unsalted* SHA-256 of the raw applicant id. ... a missing `PII_SALT` should make the dependent path fail hard, not silently degrade. ... salt = os.environ["PII_SALT"]  # raise KeyError if unset
